title: Create And Run Simple C# Console Applications - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of Console.WriteLine, Console.ReadLine, variables, data types, type casting, string formatting, string interpolation, and arrays

questions.question: What is the difference between `Console.WriteLine()` and `Console.Write()` in C#?

options.text: Console.Write() adds a newline at the end, Console.WriteLine() does not
Console.WriteLine() adds a newline at the end, Console.Write() does not
Console.Write() only accepts strings, Console.WriteLine() accepts any type

questions.explanation: `Console.WriteLine()` prints text and then moves the cursor to the next line. `Console.Write()` prints text but keeps the cursor on the same line. Both accept various data types.

questions.question: Which C# data type is used to store whole numbers (integers)?
questions.explanation: The `int` data type stores 32-bit signed integers ranging from -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647. For larger values, use `long` (64-bit). `float` stores decimal numbers, `string` stores text, and `bool` stores true/false.

questions.question: What does `Console.ReadLine()` return?

options.text: A string (the line of text entered by the user)

questions.explanation: `Console.ReadLine()` reads a line of text from the console and returns it as a `string`. To use it as a number, you must convert it using `int.Parse()`, `Convert.ToInt32()`, or similar methods.

questions.question: Which method converts the string "42" to an integer in C#?
questions.explanation: `int.Parse("42")` converts the string to an integer 42. You can also use `Convert.ToInt32("42")`. Direct casting `(int)"42"` does not work for string-to-int conversion in C#.

questions.question: What is string interpolation in C# and what is its syntax?
questions.explanation: String interpolation uses the `$` prefix before the string literal, and variables or expressions are placed inside `{}` braces. Example: `$"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."` This is cleaner than concatenation.

questions.question: How do you declare an array of integers with 5 elements in C#?
questions.explanation: C# arrays are declared with the type followed by `[]`: `int[] numbers = new int[5];` creates an array of 5 integers, all initialized to 0. You can also initialize with values: `int[] numbers = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};`

questions.question: What is the default value of a `bool` variable in C#?
questions.explanation: The default value of a `bool` in C# is `false`. All value types have default values: `int` defaults to 0, `double` defaults to 0.0, and `bool` defaults to false.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly declares a variable using implicit typing in C#?
questions.explanation: The `var` keyword lets the compiler infer the type from the assigned value. `var x = 10;` creates an `int` variable. The type is determined at compile time, not runtime, so `var` is still strongly typed.

questions.question: What is the result of: `"Hello" + " " + "World"`?
questions.explanation: The `+` operator concatenates strings in C#. "Hello" + " " + "World" joins the three strings together, resulting in "Hello World" (with the space in the middle).

questions.question: What happens if you try to access an array element at an index that is out of bounds in C#?

options.text: It returns the default value of the array type
It silently wraps around to the beginning

questions.explanation: C# performs bounds checking on arrays. Accessing an invalid index (negative or >= array length) throws an `IndexOutOfRangeException` at runtime. This is a common runtime error.
